questions.prompt: Why does longest prefix match win in routing tables?

choices.text: A more-specific prefix encodes more knowledge about the destination. If you have `0.0.0.0/0 via R1` (default) and `10.0.0.0/8 via R2`, packets to 10.5.6.7 match BOTH — but the /8 is more specific, so use R2. This lets you mix general defaults with specific overrides cleanly. Pattern: most-specific wins, ties broken by metric. Used everywhere from your laptop's routing table to BGP route selection on internet backbones. Common cases: VPN routes (10.0.0.0/8 via tunnel) override default route (0.0.0.0/0 via ISP); branch office gets specific routes to internal subnets while keeping default for internet. The 'longest prefix match' rule is universal across IPv4 / IPv6 / cloud networking; understanding it explains a lot of 'why is my traffic going here?' questions in routing tables.

questions.explanation: More-specific prefix = more knowledge about destination. Universal rule across all IP routing.

questions.prompt: Your packets to 10.5.6.7 take 12 hops but the path through hop 4 is dropping 20% of packets. Tool to spot this?

choices.text: `mtr 10.5.6.7` — combines traceroute (path discovery) with continuous ping (loss/latency per hop). A single mtr session shows: 'hop 4 has 20% loss; hops 5-12 clean.' This pinpoints exactly which link is degraded. Plain `traceroute` gives path once but no statistics — you can't tell if hop 4 is consistently dropping or just unlucky on one probe. `mtr` aggregates hundreds of probes, surfacing intermittent issues that traceroute misses. Caveat for both: loss at intermediate hops often reflects routers rate-limiting ICMP replies, not actual packet loss for normal traffic — only loss propagating through subsequent hops is genuinely affecting transit. mtr is the diagnostic tool of choice for 'why is connection X slow/lossy?' investigations.

questions.explanation: mtr = traceroute + ping per hop. Pinpoints which router/link is dropping. Standard for path diagnosis.
